sections.title: Abstract
sections.text: Dysregulated innate immune responses underlie multiple inflammatory diseases, yet clinical translation of preclinical findings is hampered by species differences between mouse and human inflammatory pathways [CITE: species differences in innate immunity between mice and humans]. In vivo models supporting functional human innate immune cells are needed to bridge this translational gap. We reconstituted NSG-QUAD mice—expressing four human myelopoiesis transgenes—with human hematopoietic stem and progenitor cells and challenged them with systemic and mucosal lipopolysaccharide. Human monocytes, dendritic cells, and granulocytes engrafted in peripheral blood, spleen, and liver by 6 weeks post-injection. Humanized NSG-QUAD mice responded to intraperitoneal and intranasal LPS with NF-κB-dependent cytokines and type I interferon, demonstrating functional pattern recognition receptor signaling. Notably, LPS challenge induced NLRP3 inflammasome-mediated IL-1β and IL-18 secretion, confirming complete inflammasome pathway functionality. The selective NLRP3 inhibitor MCC950 abrogated IL-1β and IL-18 production without affecting monocyte viability or upstream NF-κB responses, validating pathway-specific pharmacological intervention in vivo. These findings demonstrate that humanized NSG-QUAD mice support functional human innate immune responses encompassing NF-κB, type I interferon, and NLRP3 inflammasome pathways, providing a platform for mechanistic studies and preclinical validation of therapeutics targeting human inflammatory responses.

sections.title: Introduction
sections.text:
Dysregulated innate immune responses are central to the pathogenesis of sepsis, autoinflammatory syndromes, and chronic inflammatory diseases [CITE: innate immunity in inflammatory disease pathogenesis]. Pattern recognition receptors such as Toll-like receptor 4 (TLR4) detect pathogen-associated molecular patterns and activate nuclear factor-κB (NF-κB) and type I interferon signaling cascades, initiating rapid cytokine production and antimicrobial defense [CITE: TLR4 signaling to NF-κB and type I interferon]. A second tier of innate immune activation involves the NLRP3 inflammasome, a multiprotein complex that assembles in response to diverse danger signals and activates caspase-1, leading to proteolytic maturation and secretion of interleukin-1β (IL-1β) and IL-18 [CITE: NLRP3 inflammasome structure and activation mechanisms]. Excessive or prolonged inflammasome activation contributes to tissue damage in conditions ranging from acute lung injury to metabolic disease, making NLRP3 an attractive therapeutic target.

Despite extensive mechanistic insights from mouse models, clinical translation of preclinical innate immunity research is hampered by substantial species differences in immune signaling pathways, cytokine biology, and inflammasome regulation [CITE: species differences between mouse and human innate immunity]. Human in vitro systems, while valuable for dissecting molecular mechanisms, lack the cellular complexity, tissue architecture, and systemic context that characterize in vivo inflammatory responses. Humanized mouse models generated by engrafting human hematopoietic stem and progenitor cells (HSPCs) into immunodeficient recipients offer a potential solution, yet conventional strains show limited human myeloid cell engraftment and incomplete reconstitution of innate immune function [CITE: limitations of humanized mouse models for innate immunity]. The absence of human cytokines required for myeloid lineage development restricts the utility of these models for studying monocyte, dendritic cell, and granulocyte responses to inflammatory stimuli.

NSG-QUAD mice represent a next-generation humanized platform engineered to express four human cytokines—macrophage colony-stimulating factor (M-CSF), interleukin-3 (IL-3), granulocyte-macrophage colony-stimulating factor (GM-CSF), and thrombopoietin (TPO)—that support human myelopoiesis [CITE: NSG-QUAD mouse strain development and transgene expression]. We hypothesized that HSPC reconstitution of NSG-QUAD mice would generate functional human innate immune cells capable of mounting coordinated responses to pathogen-associated molecular patterns. Here, we demonstrate that humanized NSG-QUAD mice develop robust human myeloid cell engraftment and respond to lipopolysaccharide (LPS) challenge with NF-κB-dependent cytokine production, type I interferon induction, and NLRP3 inflammasome activation [CITE: LPS as a model PAMP for innate immune activation]. We assessed myeloid cell engraftment kinetics across peripheral blood, spleen, and liver, characterized cytokine responses to systemic and mucosal LPS administration, and validated inflammasome specificity using the selective NLRP3 inhibitor MCC950 [CITE: MCC950 as selective NLRP3 inhibitor]. Our results establish humanized NSG-QUAD mice as a tractable in vivo platform for studying human innate immune mechanisms and evaluating therapeutic candidates targeting inflammatory pathways.

sections.title: Results
sections.text:
Rapid Engraftment of Human Myeloid Cells in NSG-QUAD Mice.

NSG-QUAD mice reconstituted with human CD34+ hematopoietic stem and progenitor cells displayed robust engraftment of human myeloid lineages by 6 weeks post-injection. Flow cytometric analysis of peripheral blood, spleen, and liver revealed the presence of human CD45+ cells comprising monocytes, dendritic cells, and granulocytes (Figure 1A). [FILL: percentages of human CD45+ cells in each tissue and absolute cell numbers per organ]. Phenotypic characterization confirmed the presence of distinct myeloid subsets, with [FILL: surface marker profiles defining classical and non-classical monocytes, conventional and plasmacytoid dendritic cells, and neutrophil populations] (Figure 1B). These data demonstrate that NSG-QUAD mice support the development of a diverse human innate immune compartment within a clinically relevant timeframe.

HuNSG-QUAD Mice Mount NF-κB and Type I Interferon Responses to LPS.

To assess functional competence of the engrafted human myeloid cells, we challenged humanized NSG-QUAD mice with lipopolysaccharide via intraperitoneal injection. Serum analysis revealed robust induction of NF-κB-dependent cytokines, including [FILL: specific cytokines measured (e.g., TNF-α, IL-6, IL-12p70), fold-changes or absolute concentrations, and time points sampled] (Figure 2A). Notably, LPS challenge also elicited a type I interferon response, with [FILL: IFN-α and/or IFN-β concentrations and kinetics] detected in serum (Figure 2B). This observation indicates that humanized NSG-QUAD mice recapitulate both MyD88-dependent and TRIF-dependent arms of TLR4 signaling. To evaluate mucosal innate immunity, we administered LPS intranasally and observed cytokine production in bronchoalveolar lavage fluid and serum (Figure 2C). [FILL: comparison of systemic versus mucosal response magnitudes and kinetics]. These findings establish that huNSG-QUAD mice mount coordinated inflammatory responses to pathogen-associated molecular patterns via multiple anatomical routes.

NLRP3 Inflammasome Activation Produces IL-1β and IL-18.

LPS priming and activation of the NLRP3 inflammasome pathway resulted in secretion of mature IL-1β and IL-18 in humanized NSG-QUAD mice. Following intraperitoneal LPS challenge, serum levels of IL-1β and IL-18 increased [FILL: fold-change or absolute concentrations, time course, and dose-response relationship if tested] (Figure 3A, 3B). [FILL: quantitative comparison to other cytokines to demonstrate inflammasome-specific signature distinct from NF-κB outputs alone]. The kinetics of IL-1β and IL-18 secretion were consistent with a two-signal model of inflammasome activation, wherein LPS provides both priming (signal 1) and, through secondary mediators or cellular stress, activating (signal 2) stimuli. These results demonstrate that human myeloid cells in NSG-QUAD mice possess functional NLRP3 inflammasome machinery capable of processing and releasing bioactive inflammatory cytokines in vivo.

MCC950 Selectively Inhibits Inflammasome Outputs Without Affecting Cell Viability.

To validate the utility of huNSG-QUAD mice for testing inflammasome-targeted therapeutics, we administered the selective NLRP3 inhibitor MCC950 prior to LPS challenge. MCC950 treatment abrogated IL-1β and IL-18 production, reducing serum levels by [FILL: percent inhibition and statistical significance] compared to vehicle-treated controls (Figure 4A). By contrast, NF-κB-dependent cytokines [FILL: specific cytokines tested and quantitative comparison between vehicle and MCC950 groups] remained unchanged, confirming that MCC950 selectively targets inflammasome activation without disrupting upstream pattern recognition or transcriptional responses (Figure 4B). Importantly, flow cytometric analysis of splenic and hepatic monocytes revealed no difference in viability between vehicle- and MCC950-treated mice [FILL: viability percentages in each group and statistical comparison], indicating that the inhibitor does not induce cytotoxicity at the dose tested (Figure 4C). These data support the selectivity of MCC950 for NLRP3-mediated cytokine maturation and establish huNSG-QUAD mice as a platform for preclinical evaluation of inflammasome inhibitors in human immune cells.

sections.title: Discussion
sections.text:
The major findings of this study demonstrate that NSG-QUAD mice reconstituted with human hematopoietic stem and progenitor cells support rapid engraftment of functional human myeloid cells and mount complete innate immune responses to lipopolysaccharide challenge, including NF-κB activation, type I interferon production, and NLRP3 inflammasome-mediated cytokine secretion. The 6-week timeline for achieving systemic myeloid reconstitution across peripheral blood, spleen, and liver represents a substantial acceleration compared to conventional humanized mouse strains [CITE: engraftment kinetics in other humanized mouse strains], enabling more efficient experimental workflows for studying human innate immunity in vivo. Detection of monocytes, dendritic cells, and granulocytes in multiple tissues indicates that the four human myelopoiesis transgenes in NSG-QUAD mice provide sufficient instructive signals for differentiation and trafficking of human myeloid lineages [CITE: human myeloid cell development requirements in vivo].

Mechanistic investigations revealed that huNSG-QUAD mice respond to both intraperitoneal and intranasal LPS with production of NF-κB-dependent cytokines, type I interferon, and inflammasome-dependent IL-1β and IL-18, demonstrating functional maturation of multiple innate immune signaling pathways. This observation is particularly significant given well-documented species differences in Toll-like receptor 4 signaling and downstream pathway activation between mice and humans [CITE: species differences in TLR4 signaling between mice and humans]. The capacity to elicit responses via systemic and mucosal routes suggests versatility for modeling diverse infection and inflammation scenarios. Notably, MCC950 treatment specifically abrogated IL-1β and IL-18 production without affecting upstream NF-κB-dependent cytokines or monocyte viability, validating the model for testing human-specific mechanisms of NLRP3 inhibitors in vivo [CITE: NLRP3 inflammasome regulation differences across species]. These data support the utility of huNSG-QUAD mice for preclinical evaluation of inflammasome-targeted therapeutics, addressing a critical gap given the ongoing clinical development of NLRP3 inhibitors [CITE: MCC950 clinical development status].

Despite these advances, several limitations remain to be explored and elucidated. [FILL: whether T and B cell responses were assessed or develop in this model] will determine the suitability of huNSG-QUAD mice for studying innate-adaptive immune crosstalk [CITE: adaptive immune development in humanized mice]. [FILL: potential limitations of HSPC source — cord blood vs. adult bone marrow differences in myeloid differentiation capacity] may influence the functional repertoire of engrafted cells. The current study focused on LPS as a prototypical pathogen-associated molecular pattern; whether our results extend to other Toll-like receptor ligands, damage-associated molecular patterns, or particulate inflammasome activators we cannot say. [FILL: whether tissue-resident macrophages such as Kupffer cells and alveolar macrophages are of human or mouse origin] is an important consideration, as the cellular source of inflammatory mediators may affect interpretation of tissue-specific responses. Additionally, we observed variability in engraftment efficiency between individual mice, which may reflect [FILL: donor-to-donor variation in HSPC quality or recipient-specific factors affecting engraftment]. Standardization of HSPC preparation and quality control metrics will be essential for reproducible application of this model across laboratories.

Our findings serve as a foundation to accelerate a deeper understanding of human innate immune mechanisms that cannot be adequately modeled in conventional mice. HuNSG-QUAD mice provide a platform for testing inflammasome inhibitors and other innate immunity therapeutics using human cells in an in vivo context, addressing the well-recognized limitation that mouse models often fail to predict human drug responses in inflammatory diseases [CITE: limitations of mouse models for predicting human drug responses in inflammation]. The model holds particular promise for studying autoinflammatory diseases driven by inflammasome dysregulation [CITE: autoinflammatory diseases driven by inflammasome dysregulation], where patient-derived HSPCs could be used to model disease-specific mutations in a physiological environment. Beyond pharmacological studies, the system enables mechanistic dissection of human-specific innate immune pathways, including interrogation of signaling nodes that diverge between species and may account for differential disease susceptibility.

Future studies employing this model should extend characterization to additional pathogen-associated molecular patterns and damage-associated molecular patterns to fully define the innate immune competency of huNSG-QUAD mice. Combining humanized mice with human tissue grafts may enable modeling of tissue-specific inflammatory responses, such as lung inflammation in acute respiratory distress syndrome or liver inflammation in non-alcoholic steatohepatitis. The model may also prove useful for studying human-specific pathogens that do not productively infect conventional mice [CITE: human-specific pathogens requiring humanized models], providing a bridge between in vitro human cell systems and clinical investigation. Our study provides a framework to dissect heterogeneous innate immune programs in a humanized in vivo context, supporting the development of more effective and human-relevant therapeutic strategies for inflammatory diseases.

sections.title: Methods
sections.text:
Mice and Humanization. [FILL: NSG-QUAD mouse source, strain details, and housing conditions]. Human hematopoietic stem and progenitor cells were obtained from [FILL: HSPC source — cord blood, bone marrow, or mobilized peripheral blood] and isolated using [FILL: HSPC isolation method and purity assessment]. [FILL: irradiation protocol if used]. Mice received [FILL: cell dose per mouse] HSPCs via [FILL: injection route]. All animal procedures were approved under [FILL: IACUC approval number and animal welfare compliance]. NSG-QUAD mice expressing human IL-3, GM-CSF, SCF, and thrombopoietin transgenes support enhanced myeloid engraftment [CITE: NSG-QUAD strain characterization].

Flow Cytometry. Peripheral blood, spleen, and liver were processed using [FILL: tissue processing methods for blood, spleen, liver]. Cells were stained with [FILL: antibody panel — specificities, clones, fluorochromes, vendors, catalog numbers]. Human monocytes, dendritic cells, and granulocytes were identified by [FILL: gating strategy for monocytes, dendritic cells, granulocytes]. Data were acquired on [FILL: flow cytometer model and acquisition software] and analyzed using [FILL: analysis software and gating criteria].

LPS Challenge. Humanized NSG-QUAD mice were challenged with [FILL: LPS serotype, vendor, catalog number] at 6 weeks post-HSPC injection. For intraperitoneal challenge, mice received [FILL: intraperitoneal LPS dose (μg per mouse) and vehicle]. For intranasal challenge, mice received [FILL: intranasal LPS dose and administration volume]. Serum and tissues were collected at [FILL: time points for tissue and serum collection post-challenge]. Each experimental group included [FILL: number of mice per group and number of independent experiments].

Cytokine Measurement. Serum cytokines were quantified by Luminex multiplex assay using [FILL: Luminex kit vendor, catalog number, and analytes measured]. Samples were diluted and processed according to [FILL: sample dilution and assay protocol details]. Data were acquired on [FILL: instrument model and analysis software]. This approach enabled simultaneous measurement of NF-κB-dependent cytokines, type I interferon, and inflammasome-dependent IL-1β and IL-18.

MCC950 Treatment. MCC950 was obtained from [FILL: MCC950 source, purity, and formulation] and administered at [FILL: dose (mg/kg), route, and timing relative to LPS]. Control mice received [FILL: vehicle composition for control group]. Dosing was based on established pharmacokinetic parameters [CITE: MCC950 pharmacokinetics and dosing in mice].

Cell Viability Assessment. Monocyte viability was assessed by [FILL: method for assessing monocyte death — flow cytometry markers, dyes, or other assay]. Viable cells were identified using [FILL: gating strategy and viability criteria]. This analysis distinguished NLRP3 inhibition from nonspecific cytotoxicity.

Statistical Analysis. Data were analyzed using [FILL: statistical tests used for each comparison] in [FILL: software used for statistical analysis]. Significance was defined as [FILL: criteria for significance (e.g., P < 0.05)]. [FILL: correction for multiple comparisons if applicable]. All experiments included appropriate biological replicates to ensure reproducibility.
